clues.text: I was born in Ogidi, a town in Eastern Nigeria, in 1930.
My first novel was published in 1958 and has sold over 20 million copies worldwide.
I was a professor at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, where the library now bears my name.
I was a friend and contemporary of Wole Soyinka, though we often disagreed.
I am widely called the Father of African Literature.
I died in Boston in 2013 at the age of 82, but my spirit remains in my books.
I was born in Abeokuta in 1938 into a family of activists.
I invented a genre of music that blends jazz, funk, and traditional Yoruba rhythms.
My nightclub in Lagos was called the Shrine, and it was the heart of my movement.
My mother was a prominent activist whose portrait now adorns the 20 naira note.
I am remembered by the name I gave myself: Abami Eda, the Strange One.
I died in 1997 from complications of AIDS, but my music lives on as a global protest anthem.
I was born in 1954 in Ogwashi-Ukwu, Delta State.
I am the first woman and first African to lead the World Trade Organization.
I served twice as Nigeria's Finance Minister in Abuja.
I am a fellow Nigerian with Chimamanda Adichie, and we have appeared together on global stages.
Forbes named me one of the 100 Most Powerful Women in the World.
I am still serving as WTO Director-General, shaping global trade policy.
I was born in Owerri, Imo State, in 1976.
I won the UEFA Champions League with Ajax Amsterdam at age 19.
I played for Arsenal's Invincibles, the team that went an entire Premier League season unbeaten.
I captained the Nigerian national team alongside Jay-Jay Okocha.
Fans call me Papilo, a nickname that means Daddy in my native dialect.
I now run the Kanu Heart Foundation, saving children with heart defects.
I was born in Enugu in 1977 and grew up in Nsukka.
My novel Half of a Yellow Sun won the Orange Prize for Fiction in 2007.
I divide my time between Nigeria and the United States, where I teach writing workshops.
Beyoncé sampled my words on her album, introducing me to millions of new readers.
I am often called one of the most influential African writers of my generation.
I continue to write novels, essays, and speeches that shape global conversations on race and feminism.
I was born in Abeokuta in 1934, the same town Fela Kuti called home.
I wrote The Lion and the Jewel and Season of Anomy, plays performed worldwide.
I was a professor at the University of Ibadan and later taught at Harvard and Oxford.
I was imprisoned during the Nigerian Civil War and wrote my prison notes on toilet paper.
I am the first African to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
At 90, I remain a vocal critic of tyranny and an enduring symbol of African letters.
I was born in Port Harcourt in 1991 to a Nigerian mother and a British father.
My album African Giant was nominated for a Grammy, and I won the Grammy for Best Global Album in 2021.
I own a recording studio in Lagos called Spaceship Collective.
I have collaborated with Beyoncé, Ed Sheeran, and Sir David Attenborough.
I call myself the African Giant and Odogwu, a title meaning Big Man in Igbo.
I am still in my prime, touring the world and carrying Afrobeats to every continent.
I was born in Zungeru, Northern Nigeria, in 1904 to Igbo parents.
I was the first President of Nigeria after independence in 1963.
The University of Nigeria, Nsukka, which I founded, now bears my name on its library.
I led the independence struggle alongside Obafemi Awolowo and Ahmadu Bello.
I am known as Zik of Africa, a journalist turned statesman.
I died in Enugu in 1996 at the age of 91, leaving behind a legacy of pan-Africanism.
I was born in Enugu in 1973, the youngest of eight children.
I played for Paris Saint-Germain and Bolton Wanderers, dazzling fans with my skill.
I captained the Super Eagles at the 2004 Africa Cup of Nations in Tunisia.
I was teammates with Kanu Nwankwo in the Nigerian Golden Generation.
Fans called me Sojay, coined from the words SOccer and JAY.
I am now a football ambassador and mentor to young Nigerian players.
I was born in Abeokuta in 1900, into a family of educators.
I led the Abeokuta Women's Union and fought against colonial taxation of women.
My home in Abeokuta became the headquarters of the Nigerian women's movement.
I was the mother of Fela Kuti and the first woman to drive a car in Nigeria.
I am remembered as the Lioness of Lisabi for my fearless activism.
I died in 1978 from injuries sustained during a military raid on my son's compound.
I was born in Ikenne, Ogun State, in 1909.
As Premier of the Western Region, I introduced free primary education in Nigeria.
The Obafemi Awolowo University in Ile-Ife bears my name, and I was its founding vision.
I was the great rival and counterpart of Nnamdi Azikiwe and Ahmadu Bello.
I am known as Awo, a name that became synonymous with Yoruba political identity.
I died in 1987 at my home in Ikenne, and millions lined the streets for my funeral.
I was born in Sokoto in 1910, a descendant of Usman dan Fodio.
As Premier of the Northern Region, I was the most powerful politician in independent Nigeria's first decade.
The Ahmadu Bello University in Zaria, one of Africa's largest universities, carries my name.
I governed the North alongside Awolowo in the West and Azikiwe in the East.
I held the traditional title Sardauna of Sokoto, a mark of my noble lineage.
I was assassinated in the 1966 coup, shot in my home alongside my wife.
I was born in Tafawa Balewa, a district in Bauchi State, in 1912.
I became the first and only Prime Minister of independent Nigeria.
I led the nation from Lagos, the capital, during Nigeria's early years as a republic.
I worked closely with Ahmadu Bello, my mentor, who remained my senior in the North.
I was known as the Golden Voice of Africa for my eloquence on the world stage.
I was killed in the 1966 military coup, my body found on a road outside Lagos.
I was born in Umuegwu, Okigwe, in present-day Imo State, in 1916.
As Premier of the Eastern Region, I transformed the region into an industrial powerhouse.
The Michael Okpara University of Agriculture in Umudike bears my name.
I was a protégé of Nnamdi Azikiwe and led the NCNC after he became President.
I was known as the Architect of the Eastern Region for my ambitious development plans.
I went into exile after the civil war and returned in 1982 to a quiet life in Umuahia.
I was born in Okpanam, Delta State, but grew up in Kaduna, which became my adopted name.
I planned and executed Nigeria's first military coup in January 1966.
I announced the coup from Radio Kaduna, declaring the end of the First Republic.
I trained at Sandhurst alongside other young officers who would shape Nigeria's future.
I am remembered as the Ojikutu, the Vulture who struck at Nigeria's leaders.
I was killed in action near Nsukka in 1967 during the civil war I helped set in motion.
I was born in Kano in 1938, into a Hausa-Fulani family.
I became Nigeria's Head of State in a 1975 palace coup and ruled for only six months.
Lagos's international airport is named after me, the gateway for everyone who flies into the city.
I served alongside Olusegun Obasanjo, who succeeded me after my death.
History remembers me as the Iron General for my swift, decisive leadership.
I was assassinated in a failed coup attempt in Lagos in 1976, my car riddled with bullets.
I was born in Kano in 1943, a Kanuri from the north-east.
I seized power in 1993 and ruled Nigeria with an iron fist for five years.
I governed from Aso Rock, the presidential villa in Abuja, which my regime completed.
I imprisoned Olusegun Obasanjo for plotting against me, only for him to later become President.
My regime was known for its brutality and I was called the most corrupt leader in Nigerian history.
I died suddenly in 1998 at the age of 54, reportedly in the company of Indian prostitutes.
I was born in Minna, Niger State, in 1941.
I ruled Nigeria for eight years after seizing power in 1985, the longest-serving military ruler.
I built a new federal capital in Abuja, moving the seat of government from Lagos.
I annulled the June 12, 1993 election that Moshood Abiola was poised to win.
I was nicknamed Maradona for my ability to dribble around political crises.
I handed over to an interim government in 1993 and have remained a kingmaker in Nigerian politics.
I was born in Abeokuta, Ogun State, in 1937.
I was Nigeria's military Head of State and later its civilian President for eight years.
I own a farm in Otta where I retreated after leaving office, writing my memoirs.
I was imprisoned by Sani Abacha and later released to become President in 1999.
I am known simply as Baba, the father of Nigeria's modern democracy.
I remain active in African diplomacy, serving as a special envoy and elder statesman.
I was born in Otueke, Bayelsa State, in 1957, the son of a canoe carver.
I became President of Nigeria in 2010 and served until 2015.
I was the first President from the Niger Delta and took office in Aso Rock, Abuja.
I was Vice President under Umaru Yar'Adua and assumed office when he died in 2010.
I am called the Accidental President because I was never elected to the top job initially.
I conceded defeat to Muhammadu Buhari in 2015 and handed over peacefully, a first for Nigeria.
I was born in Ondo in 1946, into a royal family.
I pioneered a style of juju music that became the soundtrack of Nigerian high society.
I built a nightclub in Lagos called The Palace, where the elite gathered to hear me play.
I took juju music global and inspired bands like the Police's Stewart Copeland.
I am called the King of Juju Music, a title I earned over six decades of performance.
At nearly 80, I still perform and record, preserving the juju tradition for new generations.
I was born in Jos in 1975, but my family roots are in Benue State.
My song African Queen became one of the most beloved love songs in African history.
I rose to fame in Lagos as a member of the Plantashun Boiz before going solo.
I was a rival to fellow Nigerian musicians like D'banj and P-Square in the 2000s.
I was known as Tuface before I changed my stage name to 2Baba in 2014.
I remain active in music and philanthropy, advocating for peace and social justice.
I was born in Surulere, Lagos, in 1990, the last of six children.
My album Made in Lagos went global, and my song Essence became a worldwide hit.
I am a son of Surulere, a neighborhood in mainland Lagos that shaped my sound.
I have collaborated with Drake, Beyoncé, and Justin Bieber, putting Afrobeats on the global map.
I call myself Starboy, and that name is now the brand of my record label.
I am still at the peak of my career, selling out arenas worldwide and mentoring new artists.
I was born in Atlanta, Georgia, in 1992, but moved to Lagos as a teenager.
My single Fall became the most-watched Nigerian music video on YouTube.
I own a mansion in Banana Island, Lagos, and my record label is based in Victoria Island.
I have traded diss tracks with Wizkid, and our rivalry has defined Afrobeats for a decade.
I am known as OBO and Baddest, names I gave myself in my biggest songs.
I am at the height of my powers, headlining festivals and building a music empire.
I was born in Kaduna in 1963, but my family roots are in Kwara State.
I scored Nigeria's first-ever World Cup goal in 1994, against Bulgaria.
I played my club football in Nigeria, Ivory Coast, Portugal, Greece, and Tunisia.
I was the star striker of the Super Eagles alongside Jay-Jay Okocha and Kanu Nwankwo.
Fans called me Rubie, and my iconic celebration after scoring at the World Cup is unforgettable.
I died in 2012 in Ibadan after a battle with mental illness, mourned by a nation.
I was born in Jos in 1987, into a family of six children.
I won the UEFA Champions League with Chelsea and played in two World Cups.
I spent a decade at Chelsea in London before finishing my career in the Middle East.
My transfer from Lyn Oslo to Manchester United was hijacked by Chelsea in a famous dispute.
I was called the Hargreaves of Nigeria for my composed style in midfield.
I retired from football in 2024 and now work as a football executive and youth coach.
I was born in Azare, Bauchi State, in 1962, though my roots are in Delta State.
I captained the Nigerian national team and later coached them to win the Africa Cup of Nations.
I played club football in Belgium, France, and the United States.
I was a teammate of Rashidi Yekini and later coached Kanu Nwankwo in the national team.
I was called the Big Boss for my commanding presence as both captain and coach.
I died suddenly in 2016 in Benin City from a heart attack, just months after my wife passed.
I was born in Oguta, Imo State, in 1931, into a large family.
I published Efuru in 1966, the first English-language novel by a Nigerian woman.
I taught at the University of Lagos and later founded my own publishing house in Enugu.
I was encouraged to write by Chinua Achebe, who read my manuscript while I was at university.
I am called the Mother of African Women's Literature for my pioneering work.
I died in Enugu in 1993 at the age of 62, my legacy as a literary pioneer secure.
I was born in Minna, Niger State, in 1959, but spent my childhood in London.
I won the Booker Prize for The Famished Road, a novel that blends reality with the spirit world.
I return often to Nigeria, and my writing is deeply rooted in the country's oral traditions.
I was a contemporary of Wole Soyinka and Chinua Achebe, who paved the way for my generation.
I have been called the most imaginative of Africa's writers for my magical realism.
I continue to write poetry, novels, and essays from my base in London.
I was born in Bori, Rivers State, in 1941, into the Ogoni people.
I wrote the sitcom Basi and Company, a television show that entertained millions of Nigerians.
I led the Ogoni struggle against oil companies in the Niger Delta, my homeland.
I faced the military regime of Sani Abacha, who saw my activism as a threat.
History remembers me as a martyr for the Niger Delta and for environmental justice.
I was executed by hanging in 1995 alongside eight other Ogoni activists.
